Event: He keeps his slick, pomaded ’50s hairstyle into the early seventies
Explanation: Minor self-branding choice that keeps his persona consistent, mildly odd to others but not clearly harmful.

Event: He cannot groom his hair as usual in one episode, striking colleagues as odd
Explanation: Temporary loss of composure slightly embarrasses him while only mildly amusing or interesting others.

Event: Abigail takes Don in only because she wants a child
Explanation: She gains a wanted child but treats him poorly, harming his wellbeing.

Event: She repeatedly calls him a “whore child”
Explanation: She vents her contempt, getting emotional discharge while seriously harming his self-worth.

Event: Abigail beats young Don with a wooden spoon for “losing” his virginity to a prostitute
Explanation: She gains no real benefit and severely harms him physically and psychologically.

Event: Aimee forces herself sexually on sick adolescent Dick while he is bedridden
Explanation: She takes what she wants sexually while inflicting major trauma and no benefit to him.

Event: Abigail savagely beats Don after Aimee boasts she “took that boy’s cherry”
Explanation: Pure punitive violence; she gets no constructive gain and badly damages him.

Event: Don drinks heavily, becoming an alcoholic called out by his nurse neighbor and Allison
Explanation: Alcoholism harms his health and relationships without giving lasting benefit.

Event: He hits rock bottom in Season 6 and spends a night in jail due to drinking
Explanation: His self-destructive spree jails him and upsets others but doesn’t clearly benefit anyone.

Event: He asks Megan to track his alcohol intake
Explanation: An attempt at self‑regulation that modestly helps him and lowers burden on others.

Event: He then drinks excessively behind Megan’s back
Explanation: Deception plus relapse sabotages his own progress and hurts trust without real upside.

Event: He takes over a dead man’s identity (Don Draper) to escape his past
Explanation: Hugely improves his prospects while deceiving institutions and the dead man’s circle.

Event: He deserts the army after switching dog tags
Explanation: He protects himself and builds a new life while damaging military trust and duty.

Event: He becomes a top-tier ad executive through work and talent
Explanation: Rags‑to‑riches career greatly benefits himself and provides value and prosperity to others.

Event: He bullshits his way through a dual life to keep clients and his persona intact
Explanation: He gains status and income but sustains relationships on deceit that can hurt others.

Event: He lambasts tobacco publicly at work
Explanation: He claims moral high ground or repositions himself while materially harming tobacco accounts.

Event: He delivers an emotionally raw Hershey’s pitch revealing sincerity
Explanation: Honesty brings him little concrete gain and destabilizes his job and client relationship.

Event: He gives the impression of being a loving father early on despite being clueless
Explanation: His efforts, though inept, provide some emotional support to the kids.

Event: He later neglects his children through absence and forgetfulness
Explanation: He sacrifices their trust and his own paternal bond for short‑term impulses.

Event: He skips important meetings or behaves eccentrically because he’s “that good”
Explanation: His status grants him freedom and comfort while putting extra burden and risk on others.

Event: He constantly makes persuasive ad pitches to talk his way out of situations
Explanation: He gains money and esteem while generating campaigns that benefit clients and firm.

Event: He is willing to make ads for almost anything if the price is right
Explanation: He profits by skillfully selling products, sometimes at consumers’ expense.

Event: He treats office men’s crude behavior toward women as unacceptable and says “I have rules”
Explanation: He gains self‑respect and some moral authority while making the workplace marginally safer.

Event: He drunkenly seduces his secretary Allison and pretends nothing happened
Explanation: He gets sex and ego gratification while deeply harming her emotionally and professionally.

Event: He exercises control over Sylvia, demanding demeaning acts
Explanation: He gratifies his control needs but corrodes the relationship and harms her dignity.

Event: Sylvia breaks up with Don over his controlling behavior
Explanation: She protects herself but loses a relationship and disrupts both their lives.

Event: He treats himself as willing to make an ad for almost anything for money
Explanation: Again, profit prioritized over possible consumer manipulation or harm.

Event: He masterminds the creation of Sterling Cooper Draper Pryce to avoid McCann takeover
Explanation: He saves his own autonomy and preserves many colleagues’ jobs and culture.

Event: He later pushes to sell out to McCann when Jim Cutler threatens his place
Explanation: He secures his power and payout while exposing others to the very fate he once opposed.

Event: He repeatedly cheats on Betty while expecting her to be chaste
Explanation: He indulges himself and asserts dominance, damaging her wellbeing and trust.

Event: He lectures Sal about being faithful to his wife despite his own infidelity
Explanation: He maintains a moral façade and control while hypocritically burdening Sal.

Event: He often ignores epiphanies about valuing his wife and keeps cheating
Explanation: He sabotages his marriages, gaining only fleeting pleasure at great personal cost.

Event: He ignores Faye’s advice to address his false identity legally
Explanation: He preserves the status quo but keeps massive personal and legal risk hanging over everyone.

Event: He improvises (“Tarzan, swinging from vine to vine”) instead of planning carefully
Explanation: His risk‑taking boosts his own legend and wins but endangers the firm.

Event: He spends most vacations in California with Anna, who knows his true identity
Explanation: He gains genuine acceptance and peace, offering her closeness and support in return.

Event: He shows Adam the door when his younger brother comes seeking help
Explanation: He protects his secret and stability at the cost of Adam’s hope and support.

Event: He keeps Adam’s shoebox of keepsakes after Adam’s suicide
Explanation: Clinging to the box deepens his guilt and grief with no real benefit to anyone.

Event: He pulls strings to get the Rossen boy a draft deferment
Explanation: The boy’s life prospects improve, while Don’s reputation and access to Sylvia are enhanced.

Event: He uses the deferment to regain sexual access to Sylvia
Explanation: He secures an affair for himself while exploiting her gratitude and compromising her integrity.

Event: He settles down and marries Megan, trying to be a “ladykiller in love”
Explanation: For a time both gain affection, stability, and shared life benefits.

Event: He becomes extremely upset when Megan shows sexuality outside his control
Explanation: He asserts dominance and soothes his anxieties while stifling her autonomy and career.

Event: He manipulates Betty emotionally in their marriage
Explanation: He secures control and a stable facade while eroding her mental health.

Event: He tries to talk Betty out of divorce and threatens to cut her off financially and from the kids
Explanation: He tries to preserve his family image and control by leveraging power against her.

Event: He ultimately relents and agrees to a more amicable split with Betty
Explanation: He sacrifices some control and resources to make the divorce less damaging for all.

Event: He provides a deferment for Rossen boy specifically to regain Sylvia’s sexual favor
Explanation: He gets sexual access while using life‑changing help as leverage.

Event: He intermittently behaves as a distant but “father to his men” at work
Explanation: His guidance and advocacy help their careers while bolstering his leadership.

Event: He repeatedly abandons his children on key occasions (birthday, long trips, benders)
Explanation: He loses their trust and deepens their wounds, with no durable gain to himself.

Event: He expresses his discomfort and emotional distance from his kids honestly to another adult
Explanation: Reflecting on his feelings aids his self‑understanding and guides more empathetic parenting.

Event: He consciously chooses not to use physical punishment on his kids
Explanation: He breaks an abusive cycle, improving their upbringing and easing his own conscience.

Event: He refuses to acknowledge Abigail as his mother
Explanation: Protects his own psyche while withholding emotional recognition from someone who did raise him.

Event: He refuses to acknowledge Adam as his brother when Adam is born/young
Explanation: He shields himself from attachment and pain but wounds Adam’s sense of belonging.

Event: He often does a “Screw this, I’m outta here!” walkout from meetings
Explanation: He preserves his ego and mystique while disrespecting others’ time and process.

Event: He takes Peggy on as a sink-or-swim mentee
Explanation: His harsh mentorship ultimately gives her a powerful career and affirms his value as a teacher.

Event: He neglects office-relationship rules and starts sleeping with secretaries and coworkers
Explanation: He gratifies himself while creating emotional harm and professional complications for them.

Event: He dates Faye Miller in a relatively healthy office-adjacent relationship then sabotages it
Explanation: He destroys a potentially stabilizing partnership largely for impulse and fear.

Event: He quickly decides to marry Megan after sleeping with her as his secretary
Explanation: He gains an idealized wife but saddles her with a rushed, fragile marriage from a power-imbalanced start.

Event: He takes Lane to a Japanese monster movie
Explanation: He gains pleasant company and gives Lane rare friendship and respite.

Event: He takes Bobby to see Planet of the Apes
Explanation: Shared outing strengthens their bond and gives the child joy and experience.

Event: He responds “My God, what have I done?” after Adam’s suicide
Explanation: Realization and guilt deeply hurt him while slightly humanizing him to others.

Event: He feels similar horror learning he drove Lane to suicide
Explanation: His self‑reproach harms him more than it helps but does show moral awareness.

Event: He often calls or visits Anna, treating her as his moral anchor
Explanation: Mutual emotional support is one of his healthiest relationships.

Event: He becomes “married to the job” after breakdown, realizing advertising is what he’s good at
Explanation: He stabilizes his identity doing valuable work that also benefits the firm and clients.

Event: He goes long stretches without seeing his kids post-divorce, then takes them to flashy outings like Disneyland
Explanation: Disneyland visits give some pleasure but pattern deepens insecurity and shallow attachment.

Event: He promises to take the kids to the Statue of Liberty but never does
Explanation: He gains momentary approval while eroding long‑term trust.

Event: He has numerous “Pet the Dog” moments of kindness with his children and Anna
Explanation: Acts of warmth benefit them and give him real emotional fulfillment.

Event: He repeatedly has flings and affairs while married, nearly incapable of monogamy
Explanation: He gratifies emotional and sexual needs while inflicting sustained damage on partners.

Event: He takes a level in kindness briefly after his second marriage to Megan
Explanation: Improvement benefits his marriage and workplace relationships.

Event: He reverts to philandering between seasons 5 and 6, cheating on Megan with a neighbor
Explanation: He undermines his own fresh start for transient pleasure, harming both of them.

Event: He sincerely tries to make amends in the Season 6 finale with those he wronged
Explanation: Attempts at repair aid others somewhat but cost him his marriage and job.

Event: He self-actualizes on a hippie commune after much soul-searching, ending at peace
Explanation: He gains inner peace and clarity that position him to contribute more constructively to others.

Event: He is responsible for the creation of the famous early-’70s Coke ad
Explanation: Iconic work brings him success and provides a resonant cultural product enjoyed by millions.
